title: Why RNNs Fell Behind
concept: Sequential processing cannot be parallelized across GPU cores, making RNN training too slow for massive modern datasets.

exercises.prompt: What does the acronym RNN stand for in the abbreviation RNN stand for?
exercises.explain: RNN stands for Recurrent Neural Network, which was the standard tool for processing sequences of text before modern architectures took over.

cards.title: The Speed Barrier
cards.body: Before modern AI models like ChatGPT, we used Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). But as datasets grew massive, RNNs got stuck in the slow lane. Let's find out why they couldn't keep up with modern hardware!

cards.title: Why RNNs Fell Behind
cards.body: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) process text sequentially—one word after another. Because each step depends on the previous step, computer chips (GPUs) cannot work on multiple words at the same time. This lack of parallel processing makes training on big data very slow.

cards.title: The Assembly Line Jam
cards.body: Imagine a factory where workers assemble toy cars. An RNN is like a single-lane assembly line where Worker B cannot start painting the car until Worker A finishes building the frame. No matter how many extra workers (GPU cores) you hire, they sit around waiting because everything must be done one step at a time!

cards.title: Reading Word-by-Word
cards.body: If you want an RNN to read the sentence 'Cats love warm sunny spots', it must read 'Cats' first, update its memory, then read 'love', update again, and so on. Even if you have a powerful graphics card with 5,000 cores, only 1 core can work on this sentence at a time.

cards.title: It's Speed, Not Brains
cards.body: Many think RNNs became obsolete because they weren't smart enough. Actually, they are very capable! They fell behind simply because they are too slow to train. Modern AI needs to read billions of pages, and RNNs just take too long to do that.

cards.title: Sequential vs. Parallel
cards.body: See how processing style changes how well we can use modern computer hardware.

right.text: Processes all words at once. Uses all GPU cores simultaneously.

left.text: Processes one word at a time. Most GPU cores sit idle waiting.

exercises.prompt: Why do RNNs struggle to take advantage of powerful modern GPUs?

options.text: They do not have enough memory.
They process data step-by-step, leaving GPU cores idle.
They only work with images, not text.
They run too fast for GPUs to keep up.

exercises.explain: GPUs have thousands of cores designed to do calculations at the same time (parallelism). Because RNNs work step-by-step, they cannot utilize these cores effectively.

exercises.prompt: True or False: RNNs are great at parallel processing because they can analyze all words in a sentence simultaneously.
exercises.explain: Parallel processing allows computers to work on many words at once. RNNs cannot do this because they must process text one word at a time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of RNN bottlenecks.
exercises.sentence: RNNs are slow because their processing is ________, meaning they must wait for one word to finish before starting the next.
exercises.explain: RNNs process text in a sequential (one-after-another) order. This prevents them from processing all words at the same time.

exercises.prompt: Which system describes an RNN trying to train on a GPU?

options.text: System A: 'I process all words in a sentence at the exact same millisecond using parallel GPU cores.'
System B: 'I have to wait for the previous word's calculation to finish before I can start the next one, leaving my other GPU cores doing nothing.'
System C: 'I read text from right to left because it helps me understand grammar rules faster.'

exercises.explain: System B is the AI bot here. Standard computer code or manual tasks (like in System A and C) don't represent the sequential bottleneck of training an AI model on a GPU. System B correctly highlights how the sequential nature of RNNs wastes GPU power.

exercises.prompt: You are building a massive language model on a cluster of GPUs. Why would choosing an RNN architecture slow down your training compared to newer models?

options.text: They do not support math operations on GPUs.
They process text word-by-word, leaving GPU cores waiting.
They only work on CPUs and cannot run on GPUs at all.
They are too simple to run on modern computer hardware.

exercises.explain: RNNs process words sequentially, meaning they must wait for the current word to finish before moving to the next. This prevents them from using thousands of GPU cores at the same time, making training on giant datasets far too slow.
